company.name: Sherwin-Williams
company.description: Sherwin-Williams manufactures, develops, distributes, and sells paints, coatings, stains, applicators, and related products for professional, industrial, commercial, and consumer markets.

overview.title: Business Footprint
paragraphs: Sherwin-Williams is a large U.S.-based coatings company with a vertically integrated paint-store network, consumer brands, industrial coatings, and professional contractor relationships.

Its Sherwin-Williams branded architectural coatings are sold through a company-owned store base, while Valspar and other brands extend the portfolio into home-improvement retail and consumer channels.

overview.title: Financial Profile
paragraphs: The company reported record 2025 net sales of about $23.57 billion and gross profit of about $11.52 billion, showing a high-margin specialty materials profile rather than a commodity chemicals profile.
Its May 2026 equity value around the mid-$70 billion range and trailing P/E near 30 imply that the market values the company as a durable branded and distribution-led compounder.

moatNarrative: Sherwin-Williams' moat is built around brand trust, contractor loyalty, color systems, product specifications, formulation expertise, procurement scale, and a dense company-owned store network. Paint is physically substitutable, but professional buyers often value availability, repeatable color matching, job-site support, and warranty confidence more than lowest unit price.

The Valspar acquisition broadened the consumer and retail shelf presence, while the Paint Stores Group gives Sherwin-Williams a direct route to professional demand that many coatings manufacturers cannot easily replicate.
decentralizationNarrative: The physical chemistry of paint and coatings is decentralizable in principle: pigments, binders, fillers, additives, tinting, and local mixing can be documented and produced through smaller labs or regional microfactories. The harder parts are durability testing, substrate-specific performance, environmental compliance, color consistency, liability, and distribution trust.

Open formulations, community-scale tinting, circular materials libraries, and federated local production networks can pressure narrow parts of the market first, especially niche coatings, natural-material paints, passive-cooling coatings, and repair-oriented supply chains. They are less likely to displace Sherwin-Williams' professional specification moat quickly.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 126-150.

moat.rationale: High score reflects a company-owned professional store network, leading coatings brands, formulation expertise, large contractor relationships, and retail brand breadth through Valspar and related consumer brands.

decentralizability.rationale: Paint formulation and local tinting can be distributed, and open coating recipes exist, but professional architectural and industrial coatings require quality control, testing, regulatory compliance, warranty trust, and dependable local availability.

profitability.rationale: Sherwin-Williams reported 2025 gross profit of about $11.52 billion on about $23.57 billion of net sales, with net income of about $2.57 billion, indicating strong specialty-coatings profitability.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis reported a trailing P/E ratio of 29.87 for SHW during the May 27, 2026 trading day.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis reported Sherwin-Williams market capitalization of about $76.19 billion on May 27, 2026; this is consistent with the CompaniesMarketCap reference used by the manifest for current market-cap tracking.

products.name: Sherwin-Williams Paints
products.summary: Sherwin-Williams branded paints and coatings serve professional contractors, commercial users, industrial customers, and homeowners through a large company-owned store network.
products.whyItMatters: The product line anchors the company's professional moat because paint choice is tied to job reliability, repeat purchase behavior, color matching, local availability, and specification trust.
replacementSketch: The most realistic open replacement path starts with narrow-use coatings: documented formulas, open tint databases, community testing, and small regional production rather than a direct national-brand clone.

Local shops, maker labs, and cooperatives could use open formulations and shared quality protocols to supply standard interior coatings, specialty natural paints, or climate-adaptive coatings where performance requirements are transparent and testable.

alternatives.name: CoolPaint
alternatives.summary: CoolPaint is an open-source project for a passive radiative-cooling paint formulation made from readily available ingredients.

openness.rationale: The project explicitly publishes itself as open source and focuses on formulation sharing rather than a closed branded coating system.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The formulation is designed around accessible ingredients, making it plausible for local experimentation, but it is not a full contractor-grade paint distribution system.

readiness.rationale: CoolPaint documents promising primitive testing but remains experimental and does not claim broad commercial-grade architectural coating validation.

costLeverage.rationale: If the formulation proves durable, locally sourced ingredients and passive cooling could create cost leverage through lower material and energy costs.

alternatives.name: Fab City OS
alternatives.summary: Fab City OS and the wider Fab City network support open-source, locally adapted production and circular manufacturing infrastructure.

openness.rationale: Fab City describes an open-source hardware and shared-knowledge ecosystem, though it is a production-network framework rather than a paint-specific formula library.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The network is explicitly organized around local production, shared knowledge, and distributed collaboration across territories.

readiness.rationale: Fab City OS is infrastructure for production coordination, not a validated coatings manufacturing stack, so readiness for paint substitution is early.

costLeverage.rationale: Local production and circular material flows could reduce some logistics and waste costs, but coatings-specific savings remain unproven.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open Formulation Local Coating Labs
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federation of local coating labs could publish open formulas, run standardized durability and VOC tests, share tint recipes, and produce limited-use coatings close to demand. The concept would not immediately replace premium professional systems, but it could create pressure in commodity interior paints, specialty eco-coatings, and regional maintenance markets.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Sherwin-Williams' store and brand moat weakens where buyers can trust local test results and repeatable open formulas instead of relying on a national proprietary brand.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated production, shared test protocols, and local material sourcing; Bitcoin is not central because the hardest problem is physical quality assurance rather than settlement.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Labs publish formulas, batch records, tint curves, and test data to a shared registry; local buyers select certified producers based on geography, substrate requirements, and verified performance history.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Cheating is constrained by batch-level sample retention, third-party lab tests, open test procedures, public complaint records, and cross-lab reproducibility checks. The model remains weaker than incumbent warranty systems until enough certified batches accumulate.
failureModes: Open formulas may underperform proprietary coatings on durability, washability, mildew resistance, or substrate adhesion.
Local producers may struggle with consistent raw materials, regulatory compliance, and liability coverage.
Professional contractors may continue valuing incumbent support and warranties over lower-cost local alternatives.
adoptionPath: Start with non-critical interior coatings, passive-cooling coatings, natural paints, and community buildings where buyers accept transparent experimental performance windows.
Add shared tint libraries, third-party test panels, batch certificates, and local producer directories.
Expand into municipal, cooperative housing, and maintenance markets once reproducibility and liability models mature.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly shifts formulation, production, testing, and buyer discovery toward federated local producers.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open registries and shared testing can coordinate local labs, but coatings buyers need high trust and repeatable quality before changing purchasing behavior.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Small-batch formulation and tinting are feasible, but durable architectural coatings require controlled inputs, testing, environmental compliance, and application support.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept can pressure niche and commodity segments, but Sherwin-Williams' contractor network and professional support remain strong barriers.

products.name: Valspar
products.summary: Valspar is a consumer paint and coatings brand owned by Sherwin-Williams, with a long brand history and broad retail orientation.
products.whyItMatters: Valspar extends Sherwin-Williams into mass consumer paint purchasing, where shelf position, color choice, brand familiarity, and home-improvement retail relationships matter.
replacementSketch: Consumer paint is more vulnerable than professional specification coatings because some buyers are willing to trade brand assurance for lower cost, sustainability, local provenance, or specific performance claims.
A credible replacement would combine published recipes, community color libraries, documented safety data, and small-batch local fulfillment rather than simply copying a national retail SKU.

alternatives.name: CoolPaint
alternatives.summary: CoolPaint is an open-source passive-cooling paint project that demonstrates how a specific coating use case can be moved from proprietary product branding toward shared formulation.

openness.rationale: The project is explicitly open source and publishes a formulation-oriented approach.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Its ingredient and recipe orientation supports local experimentation, though it does not yet replace the breadth of consumer decorative paints.

readiness.rationale: The project is promising but experimental, with limited evidence of retail-grade durability, safety labeling, or consumer support.

costLeverage.rationale: Accessible ingredients and energy-saving performance could create cost leverage if durability and production repeatability improve.

disruptionConcepts.name: Community Color And Material Passport Paint
disruptionConcepts.summary: A local consumer-paint network could combine open recipes, reusable containers, neighborhood tinting, and material passports that disclose pigments, binders, VOC profile, and batch origin. Instead of competing on national advertising, the system would compete on transparency, local circularity, repairability, and verified batch history.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Valspar's retail-brand moat is most exposed where consumer buyers value transparent ingredients, local reuse loops, and refillable small-batch tinting more than national shelf branding.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is federated trust and local circular production, not Bitcoin. Shared material passports and public batch records make the network legible without requiring one central paint brand.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Local paint kitchens, reuse centers, and retailers coordinate through shared color standards, material-passport schemas, refill container tracking, and public batch directories.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Trust comes from batch labels, retained samples, basic third-party testing, public ingredient disclosures, container deposit records, and customer-visible complaint and recall histories. False sustainability claims remain a major weakness unless testing and audit rules are independent.
failureModes: Consumer buyers may not accept narrower color ranges, shorter shelf life, or visible variation between local batches.
Reused containers and locally sourced ingredients can create contamination, labeling, or regulatory risks.
The model may remain a sustainability niche if large retailers do not support refill logistics.
adoptionPath: Launch through reuse stores, community workshops, cooperative housing, and municipal repair programs focused on low-risk interior applications.
Standardize material passports, VOC disclosures, tint records, and container return deposits.
Partner with local hardware stores or maker spaces once batch quality and consumer labeling are reliable.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept distributes tinting, refill logistics, material disclosure, and batch trust across local operators.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Material passports and local circular-economy platforms can coordinate participants, but paint-specific quality and safety governance would need to be built.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Local refill and tinting are feasible for limited use cases, but consumer paint requires labeling, stability, color consistency, and contamination controls.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure sustainability-oriented consumer niches, but Valspar's retail access, brand recognition, and product breadth remain strong.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Sherwin-Williams registry bundle with sourced company metrics, two product entries, open/decentralized alternatives, and one disruption concept per product. IPO data was left null because the provided and retrieved sources did not support both IPO date and IPO market cap.
